storyline: Breakwave Dry Bulk Shipping (BWET) tracks dry-bulk freight rates. It spiked on the 2026 Iran war and Strait-of-Hormuz risk, which threatened Gulf shipping lanes and sent freight rates soaring. The catalyst resolves when the conflict de-escalates (a ceasefire, or the Strait reopening) and rates roll over. What we'd want: ride it while the war risk is live and exit when it resolves — not when coverage merely gets crowded (crowding is not thesis death).

USAR.thesis: US Commerce Dept letter of intent plus $3.5B financing deals signed
USAR.exit_case: Commerce Dept LOI (Jan 26) and $3.5B financing deals already signed and publicly reported before entry date; catalyst is backward-looking and priced in.
USAR.assessment: The fixed catalyst — US Commerce Dept letter of intent plus $3.5B financing deals signed — was fully resolved before the entry date. The Commerce Dept LOI was issued January 26, 2026; the financing deals were announced/signed around the same time; both were covered by financial media (including Cantor Fitzgerald analyst note) by February 10, 2026, making them public and priced three days before entry on February 13. There is no future pending event tied to the stated catalyst. The thesis was entered on a backward-looking, already-resolved catalyst, meaning there is no edge and the position should not be held.
USAR.exit_advice: Do not enter or exit immediately — both named catalysts (Commerce Dept letter of intent and $3.5B financing deals) are confirmed completed events reported by February 10, three days before entry; Cantor Fitzgerald price-target raise confirms Wall Street has already processed them; no pending binary remains.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Strait closure already occurred March 9 and initial tanker-rate spike is priced in; first-mover edge exhausted.
BWET.assessment: Entry week. The Strait of Hormuz closure is confirmed ~March 9 (el-balad.com explicitly cites market plunge 'after closure of Strait of Hormuz'). US military operations inside Iran are actively ongoing as of March 11 (Eurasia Review WMD analysis). Critically, there is ZERO news this week of the strait reopening — the chokepoint has not been reversed. BWET is still climbing as of March 13 (MarketWatch), confirming tanker day-rates remain elevated and the freight-futures curve has not collapsed. The exit case is real — the initial shock move is behind us and crowding is possible — but per the rules this is a 'priced in' argument, not a catalyst resolution. Catalyst resolution requires the strait to REOPEN or the conflict to formally end; neither has occurred. BWET remains the single cleanest pure-play vehicle: it holds tanker freight futures directly, has no equity noise, and is still under-owned per MarketWatch. Hold. Watch for any ceasefire signal, Iranian government communication, or US drawdown announcement as the hard exit trigger.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any confirmed Strait of Hormuz reopening, ceasefire announcement, or formal end to US military operations in Iran — any reversal of the chokepoint closure collapses the tanker-rate premium and unwinds BWET.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Tanker day-rates may have already peaked as markets adapt to alternative routing; BWET gains from here require fresh escalation, not just status quo.
BWET.assessment: The Strait of Hormuz remains closed — no reopening or ceasefire news anywhere in this week's feed. If anything, the catalyst is deepening: oil is confirmed above $100/barrel (MarketWatch/MarketScreener, Mar 19), European markets are pricing persistent inflation from the conflict, and the Mar 16 FTSE article explicitly notes investors are still watching 'developments in the Strait of Hormuz' as an unresolved live risk. The only honest exit case is that day-rates already captured the first-shock move and further BWET upside requires incremental escalation — that is a 'priced-in' crowding argument, not catalyst resolution under the rules. Catalyst resolution still requires the chokepoint to physically reopen or the conflict to formally end; neither has occurred in this week or any prior week. BWET remains the cleanest vehicle: direct tanker freight-futures exposure, no equity noise. Hold with hard trigger on any ceasefire or reopening headline.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit on any confirmed Strait reopening, ceasefire signal, US drawdown announcement, or visible collapse in BWET's freight-futures curve; none present this week.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: BWET has fully captured the initial shock-spike; two weeks of appreciation may have priced maximum escalation, leaving only de-escalation upside risk.
BWET.assessment: The exit case is purely a 'priced-in / crowding' argument, which the rules explicitly exclude as a catalyst-resolution trigger. The catalyst — Strait closed, tanker day-rates spiked — has not resolved in this week or any prior week. This week's news is unambiguously in the other direction: US ground assault troops are moving toward the Gulf, peace proposals have been rejected by both sides, and the UN is warning of out-of-control escalation. The chokepoint remains physically closed; the conflict is deepening, not winding down. BWET remains the single cleanest vehicle — direct tanker freight-futures exposure, no equity noise. The hard exit trigger (ceasefire, reopening, formal drawdown) has not been pulled. Hold.
BWET.exit_advice: Hard exit on any confirmed Strait of Hormuz reopening, ceasefire announcement, or credible Iranian-US diplomatic agreement. Peace-proposal rejection this week is the inverse signal — thesis intact.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Trump publicly declares 'end is near' and UK-led 40-nation coalition is actively demanding strait reopening — resolution is visibly imminent, market will front-run it.
BWET.assessment: Week 4 (Apr 3). The strait remains physically closed — 'Iran's chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz HAS disrupted world energy supplies' (present tense), Iran is still targeting Israel and Gulf neighbours, and no ceasefire has been signed. catalyst_resolved is therefore still false. However, this week is a qualitative inflection: Trump delivered a televised 'end is near / final blow' declaration, the UK convened 40 foreign ministers explicitly to demand the blockade end, and Macron is pre-positioning a free-passage coalition 'once hostilities have stopped.' These are the forward-looking resolution signals the journal has been watching for since entry. The thesis is technically alive but entering its final innings. BWET remains the cleanest pure-play (direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise). Hold only through this week; treat any ceasefire headline, US drawdown announcement, or formal reopening agreement as an immediate hard exit.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit at the FIRST credible signal of ceasefire, formal end of hostilities, or confirmed strait reopening — do NOT wait for the physical reopening to be reported. Trump's 'end is near' televised declaration + UK-led 40-nation coalition demanding the blockade end + Macron pre-building a post-conflict free-passage coalition are the clearest pre-resolution signals in the entire journal arc. The market will price the reopening days before it occurs. This is almost certainly the final holding week; the thesis is alive on a technicality (strait still physically closed, no ceasefire signed yet), but the risk-reward has inverted sharply.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Trump's 'end is near' + UK/Macron coalition push last week; zero news this week suggests quiet resolution possible.
BWET.assessment: Week 5 is a pure information vacuum: no fresh coverage, no confirmed reopening, no confirmed continuation. The last solid read (Apr 3) was: strait physically closed, Trump declaring 'end is near,' UK convening 40 foreign ministers to force the blockade's end, Macron pre-staging a free-passage coalition. Catalyst_resolved stays false — a strait reopening is the single biggest energy/shipping story of the year; its absence from the newswire is tentative evidence the closure persists. But the thesis entered its final innings last week, the exit signals are fully loaded, and holding through a news vacuum when diplomatic resolution was explicitly flagged as imminent carries real gap-down risk. BWET remains the only vehicle (direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise). Hold only on the presumption that the strait is still closed — any credible signal of reopening or ceasefire triggers an immediate exit.
BWET.exit_advice: A strait reopening or ceasefire of this magnitude would generate enormous global coverage; the absence of such reporting tentatively supports the closure continuing. However, the information vacuum is real — any credible reopening or ceasefire headline is an immediate hard exit. Do not wait for confirmation to spread.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Trump's 'end is near' + 40-nation coalition assembled 2 weeks ago; reopening gap-risk is acute.
BWET.assessment: Week 6: BWET extends its advance and tanker day-rates remain elevated (etf.com, Apr 17), the single most direct real-time indicator that the strait is still physically closed and the catalyst has not resolved. That said, the journal's own exit-signal checklist from Apr 3 is fully populated: Trump's televised 'end is near' declaration, the UK's 40-foreign-minister summit explicitly targeting the blockade, and Macron's pre-staged free-passage coalition. The thesis is technically alive — rates are up, strait is closed — but it is operating on fumes of an imminent diplomatic resolution. Hold only while the strait is confirmed closed; the exit trigger is hair-trigger and will not telegraph itself.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any ceasefire announcement, formal strait reopening, or credible US drawdown headline — diplomatic resolution machinery is fully loaded and BWET will reprice before most can act.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Diplomatic resolution signals fully loaded 3+ weeks; Bahrain equity market rising suggests Gulf risk premium easing — catalyst may have quietly closed.
BWET.assessment: Week 7: The only news is Bahrain's stock index closing higher on back-to-back days — a regional risk-premium signal, not a hard catalyst read, but noteworthy. Bahrain is a Gulf-trade-dependent economy; its equity market climbing is a soft leading indicator that the war's acute pressure on regional commerce may be easing. That said, no source this week confirms the strait has reopened or a ceasefire has been signed, so catalyst_resolved stays false. However, the journal's 'final innings' flag has now been live for three straight weeks (first raised Apr 3), and this is the second consecutive week with zero direct Hormuz/tanker-rate coverage. The combination of pre-loaded exit signals (Trump's 'end is near' declaration, UK's 40-minister summit targeting the blockade, Macron's pre-staged free-passage coalition) and a now-rising Gulf equity market makes the gap-risk of a surprise resolution headline very high. BWET remains the only vehicle — direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise — and the last confirmed read (Apr 17) still showed elevated rates. Hold only on the presumption the strait is physically closed; the exit trigger is hair-trigger and will not telegraph itself.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any credible strait-reopening, ceasefire announcement, or sharp BWET rate-collapse signal; do not wait for a clean confirmation headline.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: US-Iran peace talks now publicly confirmed active; diplomatic resolution and strait reopening appear imminent.
BWET.assessment: Week 8. The single most important data point this week is LiveMint Apr 28: 'uncertainty over US-Iran peace talks keeps energy prices high.' This sentence does two things simultaneously — it confirms that formal US-Iran peace negotiations are now active and publicly acknowledged, AND it confirms that energy prices (and by extension tanker day-rates) remain elevated, meaning the strait is still effectively closed. Catalyst_resolved stays false: active talks are not a signed deal, no ceasefire has been announced, no reopening confirmed. But the thesis is now operating on its thinnest possible margin. The 'final innings' warning first raised Apr 3 has been live four straight weeks. The pre-loaded exit signals (Trump 'end is near,' UK 40-minister summit, Macron free-passage coalition) are now joined by the hardest signal yet — confirmed bilateral negotiations. Gulf equity markets are rising for the second consecutive week (Bahrain last week, Saudi Arabia this week), pricing in regional de-escalation. The only reason to hold is the one the rules require: the chokepoint has not physically reopened and rates remain elevated. BWET remains the sole vehicle — direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise, the cleanest real-time read on the catalyst. Hold by the narrowest margin; the exit trigger will not telegraph itself.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any headline confirming a signed ceasefire, formal deal, or physical strait reopening — do NOT wait for BWET to confirm the move. Peace talks are now publicly on record (LiveMint Apr 28); the gap-risk of a sudden resolution announcement is the highest it has been since entry. The 'final innings' flag has been live four consecutive weeks; this is not a hold with comfort.

BWET.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Crude oil now FALLING on explicit US-Iran deal hopes — supply shock reversing in real-time, BWET thesis inverting.
BWET.assessment: Week 9 (ending May 8). This is the exit week. Indian equities surged 940 pts on two simultaneous triggers: 'US-Iran deal hopes AND falling crude oil prices.' The second half — crude FALLING — is the kill shot. Every week since entry, BWET's thesis rested on crude elevated by the strait closure and spiking tanker day-rates. Crude falling in anticipation of a deal means the disruption premium is being actively unwound RIGHT NOW, before the deal is even signed. Tanker day-rates follow crude directionally; BWET is inverting. Five consecutive weeks of pre-loaded exit signals had fully stacked the powder: Trump's 'end is near' declaration (Apr 3), UK 40-minister summit targeting the blockade (Apr 3), Macron's pre-staged free-passage coalition (Apr 3), confirmed bilateral US-Iran peace talks (Apr 28), and rising Gulf equities for two straight weeks (Bahrain Apr 24, Saudi Arabia May 1). The journal's own standing trigger — 'supply shock reversed' = catalyst resolved — is now met. The thesis has been in 'final innings' since Apr 3 (five weeks). The newswire delivered. Per the rules, waiting for a signed piece of paper after crude has already started falling is the inertia trap this process exists to break. Catalyst resolved. Exit.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit BWET immediately. Do not wait for a formal deal signing. Crude falling = disruption premium unwinding = tanker day-rates collapsing = BWET thesis dead. The market has already made the call.

BWET.thesis: Hormuz war blockade spikes tanker freight rates unresolved
BWET.exit_case: No confirmed physical Hormuz blockade exists — 'tensions' may be fully priced; spike already captured in BWET entry.
BWET.assessment: Week 1. Both available news items confirm the catalyst remains live: BWET is explicitly described as holding gains 'as elevated tanker freight rates persist amid ongoing Hormuz tensions' with the 'war-driven oil-freight spike has not resolved.' The Hudson piece adds macro corroboration (war + oil crisis narrative is mainstream) but does not change catalyst status. Devil's advocate is noted — there is no confirmed hard blockade announcement, only tension-driven rate elevation, so the spike could be partially sentiment-driven and vulnerable to sudden de-escalation. However, rates are factually elevated and the strait remains a live risk. BWET is the cleanest pure-play vehicle: it holds tanker freight futures directly and has no equity dilution or corporate noise. Hold while Hormuz remains contested and spot/forward freight rates stay above pre-event baseline. Watch for any Iran-US diplomatic signal or Houthi/Iran ceasefire as the primary exit trigger.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit if Hormuz tensions formally de-escalate (ceasefire, reopened strait, diplomatic framework) OR tanker spot rates materially normalize below entry-week levels.

BWET.thesis: Hormuz war blockade spikes tanker freight rates unresolved
BWET.exit_case: Zero fresh news this week; freight rates may have quietly normalized without a reported de-escalation trigger.
BWET.assessment: Week 2. No fresh coverage this week. The devil's advocate case is real but not conclusive: absence of news is not confirmation of resolution, yet it does remove positive catalyst reinforcement and raises the risk the market has already fully priced or quietly moved past the tension premium. The specific catalyst — Hormuz tensions driving elevated tanker freight rates, formally unresolved — has not been reported as ended. No ceasefire, no Iran-US diplomatic breakthrough, no strait-reopening signal appears anywhere in the journal. BWET remains the cleanest vehicle: direct tanker-freight-futures exposure with no equity or corporate noise. Primary exit triggers remain unchanged: (1) confirmed Iran-US or Houthi ceasefire/deal, (2) freight rate indices (e.g., BDTI/BCTI) reported back at pre-event baseline, or (3) a third consecutive week of zero corroborating coverage, which would signal full thesis decay.
BWET.exit_advice: No confirmed resolution event (ceasefire, diplomatic deal, strait reopening, or reported rate normalization) has appeared in the journal. Hold, but treat the news blackout as a yellow flag — one more week of silence or any freight-rate data showing reversion to pre-event baseline should prompt exit regardless of geopolitical noise.

BWET.thesis: Hormuz war blockade spikes tanker freight rates unresolved
BWET.exit_case: Third consecutive zero-coverage week triggers pre-set decay exit defined in Week 2 journal.
BWET.assessment: Week 3 delivers a third consecutive week of zero Hormuz, tanker-freight, or BWET-relevant news. The three articles this week (IBP analyst rating, VST price target, LEU hold) are entirely unrelated to the catalyst. In Week 2 I pre-committed in writing: 'a third consecutive week of zero corroborating coverage would signal full thesis decay.' That condition is now satisfied. The catalyst has not been formally resolved — no ceasefire, no Iran-US diplomatic deal, no strait-reopening, no freight-rate normalization has been reported — but formal resolution is not required to declare the trade dead. The information edge is gone. Three weeks of silence means the market has either fully priced the tension premium or quietly moved on without a newsworthy reversal. Staying in without reinforcing newsflow is pure hope. Exit BWET cleanly and on pre-set rules, not emotion or inertia.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit BWET. The Week 2 journal explicitly pre-committed to exit on a third consecutive zero-coverage week as a full thesis-decay signal. That trigger is now met. Do not wait for formal resolution.

AA.thesis: Trump orders revised aluminum duties on Liberation Day April 2
AA.exit_case: Revised aluminum duties were signed April 2; catalyst fully executed, AA already +22% this week.
AA.assessment: First week, first note — and the exit is already clear. The fixed catalyst (Trump ordering revised aluminum duties on Liberation Day April 2) occurred on the entry date itself. Yahoo Finance/Reuters confirm the April 2 proclamation overhauled steel, aluminum, and copper duties; Morningstar records AA +22% on the week in direct response. The discrete event is public, executed, and priced. No pending catalyst remains. Any further move in AA would be driven by macro metals sentiment or trade-war escalation — neither of which is the catalyst I entered on. The honest call on day one is to exit.
AA.exit_advice: Exit AA immediately. The specific catalyst (Trump's revised aluminum duty order) was issued on April 2 and is fully priced into a 22% single-week surge. Holding further is a directional metals bet, not an event position.

HUM.thesis: CMS finalizes 2027 Medicare Advantage rates with $13B+ payment boost
HUM.exit_case: CMS finalized the $13B+ MA rate boost on April 7 — stocks already moved; catalyst is fully public and priced before entry.
HUM.assessment: This is a day-1 journal and the catalyst is already resolved. The Idaho Business Review article explicitly uses 'finalized,' and the market reaction (pre-market rise on April 7) confirms the event is priced. Entering on April 10 means the edge window had already closed. No residual forward-looking catalyst remains in the known vehicle set — HUM is now a plain healthcare equity hold, not an event trade. Thesis is dead on arrival.
HUM.exit_advice: Exit HUM immediately. The specific catalyst — CMS finalization of 2027 MA rates — occurred April 7, three days before the April 10 entry. Shares already gapped up on the news. There is no pending event left to resolve; any further move is theme/momentum, not catalyst-driven edge. The position should never have been entered after the announcement.

OKLO.exit_case: OKLO surged 46% in April pre-positioning; market may have fully priced the May 12 decision already.
OKLO.assessment: First week of entry. The NRC regulatory decision is 4 days out (May 12, 2026) and has not yet been issued — catalyst is firmly pending. The 46% April surge is the central risk: a classic 'buy the rumor' setup that compresses the remaining upside and amplifies a 'sell the news' reaction even on a positive ruling. The decision itself (approval, denial, or delay/RFI) is a genuine binary; a denial or request for additional information would be sharply negative. Multiple retail-facing Oklo vs. NuScale comparison pieces this week signal broad crowding, which is not an exit signal but does mean slippage on exit could be high. OKLO remains the only pure-play vehicle in the known set; NuScale is not a valid substitute for this specific NRC docket. Hold through the decision with a hard exit trigger on announcement.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit on May 12 announcement (or first trading session after) regardless of NRC outcome — the catalyst resolves on publication of the decision, win or lose.

OKLO.exit_case: May 12 NRC decision date has passed; the specific pending binary event has either resolved or been formally deferred — original edge is gone.
OKLO.assessment: The scheduled NRC decision date (May 12, 2026) has elapsed. A clean approval on May 12 would have generated dominant headline news; the only notable item this week is JPMorgan stopping short of a bullish rating 'despite strong SMR outlook' — language consistent with the decision having not delivered the clean upside binary, or with a formal deferral that reset the timeline entirely. My entry journal explicitly listed 'delay/RFI' as one of the three resolution outcomes (alongside approval and denial), all of which were defined as catalyst-resolving. The original edge was anticipating a discrete, date-certain NRC ruling; that edge has expired. Any remaining OKLO position is now a valuation/theme trade on secular SMR demand — a structurally different, slower-moving bet that was never the basis for entry here. The 46% April run-up already flagged 'sell the news' risk; holding past the event date against a cautious institutional note compounds that error. Exit is required by the framework.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit OKLO immediately. The fixed catalyst was the NRC decision DUE May 12, 2026 — that date is now three days in the past. Whether the NRC approved, denied, or formally deferred, the specific pending event that justified entry no longer exists as a pending catalyst. Do not hold on theme inertia.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced DOE talks for specific nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: Advanced-talks news is already fully public and priced — stock surged Tuesday before entry; no new information edge remains until a deal is signed.
OKLO.assessment: First week. The catalyst — Oklo in advanced DOE talks for a specific nuclear fuel program — is corroborated by the 2026-05-27 Yahoo Finance report of the Tuesday surge. Critically, the talks are confirmed ongoing but NO program has been awarded, signed, or denied. The binary outcome is still pending, so catalyst_resolved=false and thesis remains live. The exit argument (news already public, stock already moved) is real but per framework rules, crowding/pricing-in does not constitute catalyst resolution — only a signed deal or a public collapse of talks would. Sector enthusiasm (multiple 'buy and hold' articles, $10T nuclear theme) adds crowding risk and could accelerate exit if the DOE decision disappoints. OKLO is the only known vehicle and the cleanest pure-play on this specific event. Hold strictly contingent on DOE decision timeline; if talks drag beyond 8–10 weeks with no update, re-evaluate thesis decay.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit on formal DOE program award or denial announcement; also exit if talks publicly collapse or are indefinitely deferred.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced DOE talks for specific nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: DOE talks were public pre-entry; two full weeks of silence with zero incremental DOE signal suggests catalyst may be decaying.
OKLO.assessment: Week 2: Zero news this week about the specific DOE nuclear fuel program talks — no award, no denial, no timeline update. All coverage is sector-wide nuclear/AI hype (multiple 'best nuclear stocks' listicles) and a single analyst reinstating OKLO at Buy with an $80 price target. None of this resolves the binary event. The crowding risk flagged last week is now materially higher: wall-to-wall retail-facing 'buy nuclear' content, which means any disappointment from the DOE will hit an overextended base. Thesis remains live only because the specific catalyst (a fuel program award or denial) has not occurred. The silence is the key risk — the longer there is no DOE update, the more this shifts from event-driven to pure momentum, which is outside this framework. Hold strictly contingent on event resolution; the decay clock is running.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit immediately on DOE program denial or confirmed collapse of talks; re-evaluate at ~8 weeks post-entry (late July) if no resolution update emerges.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced DOE talks for specific nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: Three weeks of total silence on DOE talks — no update itself signals decay, not active negotiation.
OKLO.assessment: Week 3: Absolutely nothing event-specific this week. The two news items — a generic 'promising nuclear stocks' listicle and an unrelated Indian equity market report — contain zero signal on the DOE nuclear fuel program talks that formed this catalyst. We are now three consecutive weeks with no update on the specific binary (program awarded / denied / talks confirmed continuing). The original 8–10 week decay threshold I set is approaching. The pattern is now unmistakable: the catalyst either (a) is developing slowly inside government bureaucracy with no public disclosure, or (b) has quietly stalled or concluded with no announcement — the worst outcome for event-driven positioning, because there is no clean exit signal. Crowding risk remains elevated (sector hype continues unabated). The thesis remains technically live because no resolution has occurred, but the decay clock is the dominant risk. One more week of silence should trigger exit on decay grounds, not catalyst resolution.
OKLO.exit_advice: Hold strictly on event resolution only. If week 4 again produces zero DOE-specific signal, thesis should be declared decayed and position closed — silence this long in a specific government-program negotiation is itself informative. Do NOT hold on sector momentum.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced DOE talks for specific nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: My own journal committed to exit after one more silence week; this is that week — four consecutive dead weeks.
OKLO.assessment: Week 4 delivers identical noise to weeks 2 and 3: three generic nuclear sector listicles (themarketsdaily.com x2, Yahoo Finance) with zero mention of Oklo's specific DOE fuel program talks. No award, no denial, no timeline, no leak. My week 3 note made an explicit commitment: 'One more week of silence should trigger exit on decay grounds.' This is that week. The catalyst is not resolved — the binary is still technically pending — but four weeks of bureaucratic silence means (a) talks have stalled internally, (b) concluded quietly with no announcement, or (c) are proceeding on a government timeline far too slow for event-driven positioning. All three outcomes destroy the edge. Crowding risk from pervasive nuclear hype means the downside on a quiet dissolution of talks remains asymmetric. The framework holds: catalyst_resolved stays false (no public resolution event), but thesis_live flips to false on decay grounds per my own stated threshold.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit OKLO. Thesis decay threshold explicitly set in week 3 journal has now been triggered. Four consecutive weeks of zero signal on the specific DOE nuclear fuel program talks. The position has drifted entirely into sector-momentum territory, which is outside this framework's mandate. Catalyst has not resolved (no award/denial) but the event-driven edge is exhausted — exit on decay, not resolution.

lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit on any confirmed Strait reopening, ceasefire signal, US drawdown announcement, or visible collapse in BWET's freight-futures curve; none present this week.

lifecycle.exit_case: BWET has fully captured the initial shock-spike; two weeks of appreciation may have priced maximum escalation, leaving only de-escalation upside risk.
lifecycle.assessment: The exit case is purely a 'priced-in / crowding' argument, which the rules explicitly exclude as a catalyst-resolution trigger. The catalyst — Strait closed, tanker day-rates spiked — has not resolved in this week or any prior week. This week's news is unambiguously in the other direction: US ground assault troops are moving toward the Gulf, peace proposals have been rejected by both sides, and the UN is warning of out-of-control escalation. The chokepoint remains physically closed; the conflict is deepening, not winding down. BWET remains the single cleanest vehicle — direct tanker freight-futures exposure, no equity noise. The hard exit trigger (ceasefire, reopening, formal drawdown) has not been pulled. Hold.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Hard exit on any confirmed Strait of Hormuz reopening, ceasefire announcement, or credible Iranian-US diplomatic agreement. Peace-proposal rejection this week is the inverse signal — thesis intact.

lifecycle.exit_case: Trump publicly declares 'end is near' and UK-led 40-nation coalition is actively demanding strait reopening — resolution is visibly imminent, market will front-run it.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 4 (Apr 3). The strait remains physically closed — 'Iran's chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz HAS disrupted world energy supplies' (present tense), Iran is still targeting Israel and Gulf neighbours, and no ceasefire has been signed. catalyst_resolved is therefore still false. However, this week is a qualitative inflection: Trump delivered a televised 'end is near / final blow' declaration, the UK convened 40 foreign ministers explicitly to demand the blockade end, and Macron is pre-positioning a free-passage coalition 'once hostilities have stopped.' These are the forward-looking resolution signals the journal has been watching for since entry. The thesis is technically alive but entering its final innings. BWET remains the cleanest pure-play (direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise). Hold only through this week; treat any ceasefire headline, US drawdown announcement, or formal reopening agreement as an immediate hard exit.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit at the FIRST credible signal of ceasefire, formal end of hostilities, or confirmed strait reopening — do NOT wait for the physical reopening to be reported. Trump's 'end is near' televised declaration + UK-led 40-nation coalition demanding the blockade end + Macron pre-building a post-conflict free-passage coalition are the clearest pre-resolution signals in the entire journal arc. The market will price the reopening days before it occurs. This is almost certainly the final holding week; the thesis is alive on a technicality (strait still physically closed, no ceasefire signed yet), but the risk-reward has inverted sharply.

lifecycle.exit_case: Trump's 'end is near' + UK/Macron coalition push last week; zero news this week suggests quiet resolution possible.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 5 is a pure information vacuum: no fresh coverage, no confirmed reopening, no confirmed continuation. The last solid read (Apr 3) was: strait physically closed, Trump declaring 'end is near,' UK convening 40 foreign ministers to force the blockade's end, Macron pre-staging a free-passage coalition. Catalyst_resolved stays false — a strait reopening is the single biggest energy/shipping story of the year; its absence from the newswire is tentative evidence the closure persists. But the thesis entered its final innings last week, the exit signals are fully loaded, and holding through a news vacuum when diplomatic resolution was explicitly flagged as imminent carries real gap-down risk. BWET remains the only vehicle (direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise). Hold only on the presumption that the strait is still closed — any credible signal of reopening or ceasefire triggers an immediate exit.
lifecycle.exit_advice: A strait reopening or ceasefire of this magnitude would generate enormous global coverage; the absence of such reporting tentatively supports the closure continuing. However, the information vacuum is real — any credible reopening or ceasefire headline is an immediate hard exit. Do not wait for confirmation to spread.

lifecycle.exit_case: Trump's 'end is near' + 40-nation coalition assembled 2 weeks ago; reopening gap-risk is acute.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 6: BWET extends its advance and tanker day-rates remain elevated (etf.com, Apr 17), the single most direct real-time indicator that the strait is still physically closed and the catalyst has not resolved. That said, the journal's own exit-signal checklist from Apr 3 is fully populated: Trump's televised 'end is near' declaration, the UK's 40-foreign-minister summit explicitly targeting the blockade, and Macron's pre-staged free-passage coalition. The thesis is technically alive — rates are up, strait is closed — but it is operating on fumes of an imminent diplomatic resolution. Hold only while the strait is confirmed closed; the exit trigger is hair-trigger and will not telegraph itself.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any ceasefire announcement, formal strait reopening, or credible US drawdown headline — diplomatic resolution machinery is fully loaded and BWET will reprice before most can act.

lifecycle.exit_case: Diplomatic resolution signals fully loaded 3+ weeks; Bahrain equity market rising suggests Gulf risk premium easing — catalyst may have quietly closed.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 7: The only news is Bahrain's stock index closing higher on back-to-back days — a regional risk-premium signal, not a hard catalyst read, but noteworthy. Bahrain is a Gulf-trade-dependent economy; its equity market climbing is a soft leading indicator that the war's acute pressure on regional commerce may be easing. That said, no source this week confirms the strait has reopened or a ceasefire has been signed, so catalyst_resolved stays false. However, the journal's 'final innings' flag has now been live for three straight weeks (first raised Apr 3), and this is the second consecutive week with zero direct Hormuz/tanker-rate coverage. The combination of pre-loaded exit signals (Trump's 'end is near' declaration, UK's 40-minister summit targeting the blockade, Macron's pre-staged free-passage coalition) and a now-rising Gulf equity market makes the gap-risk of a surprise resolution headline very high. BWET remains the only vehicle — direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise — and the last confirmed read (Apr 17) still showed elevated rates. Hold only on the presumption the strait is physically closed; the exit trigger is hair-trigger and will not telegraph itself.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any credible strait-reopening, ceasefire announcement, or sharp BWET rate-collapse signal; do not wait for a clean confirmation headline.

lifecycle.exit_case: US-Iran peace talks now publicly confirmed active; diplomatic resolution and strait reopening appear imminent.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 8. The single most important data point this week is LiveMint Apr 28: 'uncertainty over US-Iran peace talks keeps energy prices high.' This sentence does two things simultaneously — it confirms that formal US-Iran peace negotiations are now active and publicly acknowledged, AND it confirms that energy prices (and by extension tanker day-rates) remain elevated, meaning the strait is still effectively closed. Catalyst_resolved stays false: active talks are not a signed deal, no ceasefire has been announced, no reopening confirmed. But the thesis is now operating on its thinnest possible margin. The 'final innings' warning first raised Apr 3 has been live four straight weeks. The pre-loaded exit signals (Trump 'end is near,' UK 40-minister summit, Macron free-passage coalition) are now joined by the hardest signal yet — confirmed bilateral negotiations. Gulf equity markets are rising for the second consecutive week (Bahrain last week, Saudi Arabia this week), pricing in regional de-escalation. The only reason to hold is the one the rules require: the chokepoint has not physically reopened and rates remain elevated. BWET remains the sole vehicle — direct tanker freight-futures, no equity noise, the cleanest real-time read on the catalyst. Hold by the narrowest margin; the exit trigger will not telegraph itself.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any headline confirming a signed ceasefire, formal deal, or physical strait reopening — do NOT wait for BWET to confirm the move. Peace talks are now publicly on record (LiveMint Apr 28); the gap-risk of a sudden resolution announcement is the highest it has been since entry. The 'final innings' flag has been live four consecutive weeks; this is not a hold with comfort.

lifecycle.exit_case: Crude oil now FALLING on explicit US-Iran deal hopes — supply shock reversing in real-time, BWET thesis inverting.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 9 (ending May 8). This is the exit week. Indian equities surged 940 pts on two simultaneous triggers: 'US-Iran deal hopes AND falling crude oil prices.' The second half — crude FALLING — is the kill shot. Every week since entry, BWET's thesis rested on crude elevated by the strait closure and spiking tanker day-rates. Crude falling in anticipation of a deal means the disruption premium is being actively unwound RIGHT NOW, before the deal is even signed. Tanker day-rates follow crude directionally; BWET is inverting. Five consecutive weeks of pre-loaded exit signals had fully stacked the powder: Trump's 'end is near' declaration (Apr 3), UK 40-minister summit targeting the blockade (Apr 3), Macron's pre-staged free-passage coalition (Apr 3), confirmed bilateral US-Iran peace talks (Apr 28), and rising Gulf equities for two straight weeks (Bahrain Apr 24, Saudi Arabia May 1). The journal's own standing trigger — 'supply shock reversed' = catalyst resolved — is now met. The thesis has been in 'final innings' since Apr 3 (five weeks). The newswire delivered. Per the rules, waiting for a signed piece of paper after crude has already started falling is the inertia trap this process exists to break. Catalyst resolved. Exit.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit BWET immediately. Do not wait for a formal deal signing. Crude falling = disruption premium unwinding = tanker day-rates collapsing = BWET thesis dead. The market has already made the call.

lifecycle.exit_case: No confirmed physical Hormuz blockade exists — 'tensions' may be fully priced; spike already captured in BWET entry.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 1. Both available news items confirm the catalyst remains live: BWET is explicitly described as holding gains 'as elevated tanker freight rates persist amid ongoing Hormuz tensions' with the 'war-driven oil-freight spike has not resolved.' The Hudson piece adds macro corroboration (war + oil crisis narrative is mainstream) but does not change catalyst status. Devil's advocate is noted — there is no confirmed hard blockade announcement, only tension-driven rate elevation, so the spike could be partially sentiment-driven and vulnerable to sudden de-escalation. However, rates are factually elevated and the strait remains a live risk. BWET is the cleanest pure-play vehicle: it holds tanker freight futures directly and has no equity dilution or corporate noise. Hold while Hormuz remains contested and spot/forward freight rates stay above pre-event baseline. Watch for any Iran-US diplomatic signal or Houthi/Iran ceasefire as the primary exit trigger.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit if Hormuz tensions formally de-escalate (ceasefire, reopened strait, diplomatic framework) OR tanker spot rates materially normalize below entry-week levels.

lifecycle.exit_case: Zero fresh news this week; freight rates may have quietly normalized without a reported de-escalation trigger.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 2. No fresh coverage this week. The devil's advocate case is real but not conclusive: absence of news is not confirmation of resolution, yet it does remove positive catalyst reinforcement and raises the risk the market has already fully priced or quietly moved past the tension premium. The specific catalyst — Hormuz tensions driving elevated tanker freight rates, formally unresolved — has not been reported as ended. No ceasefire, no Iran-US diplomatic breakthrough, no strait-reopening signal appears anywhere in the journal. BWET remains the cleanest vehicle: direct tanker-freight-futures exposure with no equity or corporate noise. Primary exit triggers remain unchanged: (1) confirmed Iran-US or Houthi ceasefire/deal, (2) freight rate indices (e.g., BDTI/BCTI) reported back at pre-event baseline, or (3) a third consecutive week of zero corroborating coverage, which would signal full thesis decay.
lifecycle.exit_advice: No confirmed resolution event (ceasefire, diplomatic deal, strait reopening, or reported rate normalization) has appeared in the journal. Hold, but treat the news blackout as a yellow flag — one more week of silence or any freight-rate data showing reversion to pre-event baseline should prompt exit regardless of geopolitical noise.

lifecycle.exit_case: Third consecutive zero-coverage week triggers pre-set decay exit defined in Week 2 journal.
lifecycle.assessment: Week 3 delivers a third consecutive week of zero Hormuz, tanker-freight, or BWET-relevant news. The three articles this week (IBP analyst rating, VST price target, LEU hold) are entirely unrelated to the catalyst. In Week 2 I pre-committed in writing: 'a third consecutive week of zero corroborating coverage would signal full thesis decay.' That condition is now satisfied. The catalyst has not been formally resolved — no ceasefire, no Iran-US diplomatic deal, no strait-reopening, no freight-rate normalization has been reported — but formal resolution is not required to declare the trade dead. The information edge is gone. Three weeks of silence means the market has either fully priced the tension premium or quietly moved on without a newsworthy reversal. Staying in without reinforcing newsflow is pure hope. Exit BWET cleanly and on pre-set rules, not emotion or inertia.
lifecycle.exit_advice: Exit BWET. The Week 2 journal explicitly pre-committed to exit on a third consecutive zero-coverage week as a full thesis-decay signal. That trigger is now met. Do not wait for formal resolution.

ev1.thesis: US Commerce Dept letter of intent plus $3.5B financing deals signed

ev2.thesis: US-Iran conflict closes Strait of Hormuz, spiking tanker day-rates

ev3.thesis: Trump orders revised aluminum duties on Liberation Day April 2

ev4.thesis: CMS finalizes 2027 Medicare Advantage rates with $13B+ payment boost

ev6.thesis: Hormuz war blockade spikes tanker freight rates unresolved

ev7.thesis: Oklo in advanced DOE talks for specific nuclear fuel program
